I don't remember the person off the top of my head, but basically just saying why he prefers the term context engineering over prompt engineering. And I feel like after this tweet went out, everyone was talking about context engineering. How Andre Karpathy defined the term is the delicate art and science of filling the context window with just the right information for the next step. The key idea here is the context window. The context window is all the text that we want the LLM to process in generating its response. So we can think of it as like this blank page, this window of information. And in the context window, we're going to have things like our system message. These are going to be instructions for what we want our LLM system to do. Maybe some guidance on how to use tools, especially as now LLMs have become more and more agentic. Maybe some examples of how to perform the specific task that we want it to do. References. Let's say we have some kind of retrieval system. LLMs know a lot, but they don't know everything. So sometimes we want it to fetch domain specific or organization specific information. And then memory. So let's say we want our system to have a long-term memory of interactions with a user. So we can store that in the system message as well. But there's a lot more to the context window. We're also going to have like tool metadata again as LLMs become more and more agentic. We'll have things like user messages. We'll have the assistant messages, so how the LLM is responding. And we'll have many assistant and user message pairs. We'll even have tool calls. We'll have tool call results. And on and on and on. A lot of stuff goes in the context window. Anytime you're making an LLM request, all this text is getting passed to the LLM and it's generating tokens as a response. With early versions of large language models, context window was a big constraint. But now, context windows are getting massive. GPT5 has a context window of 128,000 tokens. That's approximately the length of a textbook, like a university textbook. And that's on the smaller end. So, we have claw 3.7 sonnet, which has about 200,000. I think Claude 4 is probably about the same if not a little bigger. We have Gemini 2.5 which has a relatively big context window with a million tokens. So that's about seven and a half textbooks. And then Llama 4 has by far the biggest context window that can fit about 75 textbooks worth of text. Context engineering is all about managing this context window. A natural question might come up. I see James in the chat. What's the difference between prompt engineering and context engineering? just like a common confusion point for a lot of people these days. And I think it is for good reason because what people now call context engineering is what people have been calling prompt engineering for the past 2 or 3 years. But now we're starting to see like a distinction between these two things that we do when building LLM systems. To just put it simply, if this is our entire context window, what people call prompt engineering now is mainly going to be focused on this part. Crafting the system message, crafting the instructions. So basically giving the LLM clear instructions on doing a specific task or solving a specific problem. While context engineering is a bit broader. It's not just about crafting the system message. It's about crafting this entire context window and even having a dynamic context window. So maybe you're going to be adding and removing specific examples or references. Maybe you'll be summarizing user and assistant message pairs. Maybe you'll be removing tool calls or different types of metadata throughout. You can get as sophisticated as you want. And we'll talk a little bit more about this a little later. Now that we've talked about what context engineering is and how it's different from prompt engineering, let's talk about five practical tips for doing it. So the five tips here are going to be writing clear instructions, using structured text, keeping your context window tidy, which we'll talk about why in a second, running experiments, and then finally metaprompting. Starting with the first one, writing clear instructions. This is essentially what goes into prompt engineering. So you'll see a lot of overlap between the tips and skills you need for good context engineering and good prompt engineering. Here what we're talking about is telling the model what to do and how to do it. A good heristic here is when you're writing these instructions like your system message or whatever it might be. Ask yourself how would you describe this task to a competent and smart intern. For example, let's say I want an intern to do something for me in my business. Let's say it's like a pretty simple task. So I don't really need to give it a whole lot of guidance and instruction. If I just wanted to like rename some files in a specific folder, I can just plainly ask it to do that and then a competent intern will be able to do this. However, if I'm doing something a bit more specific, like let's say updating a weekly sales report, but it needs to be updated in a very specific way and the data is living in a specific place and new data is coming from multiple sources. Maybe I'm going to need some kind of stepby-step and clear checklist for an intern to do this. Those are the same steps you'd want to give to a large language model and doing the same task. There's going to be like the spectrum here. Very simple tasks where you don't need a whole lot of detail. You can keep your prompts, your instructions short, but for more specialized tasks that might be more domain specific or organization specific, the prompts are going to get longer and longer. Here are a few things that you might want to include in your instructions or your system messages. While there might be some variation from like different models, these are just generally helpful components to think about. First thing is a role. Basically telling the LLM what it is acting as. For example, if we're trying to get the model to write copy for us, we can say you are a senior copywriter at a boutique AI consulting firm. Also, you want to tell it its objective. So, what is the model supposed to do? For example, we'll say review and suggest improvements to marketing copy provided by the user. If the objective is pretty open-ended or you want the model to achieve the objective in a very specific way, you might want to give it explicit steps. So, what are the specific steps the model should take to complete the task? In this case, maybe what we want is the model to identify the core message, suggest one to do rewrites, and then explain its rationale. This is also overlapping with so-called chain of thought reasoning. Basically, the more tokens that your LLM generates, the better performance it's going to have. This is just a way to force the LLM to generate more tokens. Another thing is rule. So, guidelines for what the model should do and what the model should not do. An example here is like keep the tone professional, concise, avoid jargon, filler words, and emojis. Other very powerful things are putting examples. So, demonstrations of how the model should respond in different situations. For example, if we have an input of turn AI hype into real business results, we'll want to give examples of exactly how we want the model to respond. So, it'll be like number one, the core message, and then it'll have what the core message is. Then, it'll go to the one to two rewrites, and then it'll go to explaining the rationale references. This is helpful content for achieving the objective. So, if this is a copywriter for a consulting firm, maybe we'll have like about the firm. Maybe we'll have like a set of past use cases. Maybe we'll have a set of customer testimonials. All these different things are going to be helpful for the LLM to do this task. And then finally, tool selection. You know, if you're building an agentic system and you're allowing the LLM to select tools from some tool set, you might want to include special instructions for when and how to use specific tools. If we have this reading level tool, we might include in our system message, use the reading level tool to check the readability score. All copy should be an eighth grade reading level or lower. These are just a handful of components. Of course, there might be other things you want to include in your system prompt, but if you just have these, you can basically get the LLM to do anything. The next tip is to use structured text. What I mean by this is using things like markdown and XML in your context window. Markdown is a markup language and allows you to basically format text. So, one hashtag is header one, two hashtags is header two, and so on. You can have unordered lists. You can have numbered lists. You can bold text. You can italicize text. You can have like specialized code blocks, Python blocks, JSON blocks, or just generic blocks of text that are distinct from other sections. So, this is just an easy way to break down complex instructions. If you have a lot going on in your system message or in the context window, adding markdown is just an easy way to delineate different sections. And just like for us, it's easier to read a well- formatted PDF. It's easier for LLMs to parse well organized context windows as opposed to notes that don't really have much order to it. Another thing we can do to add structure to our context window is using XML. XML is basically adding these tags to different parts of your prompt or context. So a simple example is if you want to give specific examples of the task, you can have like a start example tag and an end example tag and then just put the examples in between those tags. This is helpful especially if you want to use markdown in the example. Then it's going to be easier for the model to distinguish it if it's between two XML tags than if it's blending in with all the other markdown formatting. Another place where this is powerful is if you're injecting references. You do some kind of retrieval step. you fetch some chunks or snippets, you can inject it into the context window and you can add metadata. So you can say like this is from document number one and it's from page number 47 or whatever arbitrary metadata you want to add to the XML tag. The two popular use cases for XML is to delimmit task examples and source documents. Also, GPT5 seems like you can control different behaviors or properties of the model's outputs, like its verbosity using XML tags. This is going to change from model to model, but broadly speaking, you can add this structured text to any model, and this should be helpful. Tip three is to keep it tidy. So, basically, give the model everything it needs, but nothing more. A good reason for this came from a recent paper or report from Chroma DB. They introduced this term of context rot which is basically this idea that model performance tends to degrade with longer contexts and irrelevant information. Here's a plot that kind of shows this. They basically had this very simple task of telling an LLM to repeat some chunk of text basically copy paste text over and over again which is like a trivial task for a computer to do. But we can see as the context length gets longer and longer all the models basically just fall apart. Cloud 4 seems to hold up the best, but it eventually too just tanks in performance. GPT4.1 and Gemini 2.5 start dropping pretty early on and then Quen is kind of in between those different models. This is a really important point because people were saying like, oh, Rag is dead or, you know, X is dead or whatever. But there's still this fact of like even though we have these bigger and bigger context windows, that doesn't mean you can just shove all the text you want into them and get the same amount of performance. you're going to have this drop in performance that you have to deal with. There are many things you can do to deal with this, but a couple suggested by Chroma is summarizing past chats. So, if you have like these user message assistant message pairs after a certain point, if they're like days old or X messages old, you can just summarize them and then have that in the context as opposed to all these raw messages. Another thing you could do is one, use a retrieval system, but two, if you are using some kind of rag system, focus on improving the retrieval results first and in isolation because the less irrelevant information you're injecting into the context window, the better performance you're going to get, especially as your system scales. Tip number four is to run experiments. Basically, this is the idea of evaluating whether your LLM app actually does what you want. There are two key ingredients for running experiments like this. The first is knowing the desired behavior that you want your app to do. The second is comparing the actual behavior to the desired behavior. So some way to evaluate the systems performance. The way I see it, this kind of happens at multiple scales and these are like the three most common levels. The first level is what most people do. What we can call like ad hoc checks or vibe checks. So this is basically you're in chatbt or in some kind of playground and you work on your prompt. You see what the model spits back as a response. you don't really like it. So you go back and you edit your prompts. You see how that performs. It's a little bit better. But then you try to give it a edge case, see how it handles that. And you just kind of go through this iterative loop a handful of times. So this is pretty good, but it's not going to get you like amazing performance because you're not going to be looking at a whole lot of examples. The second level of this is a systematic manual review of a bunch of examples here. Instead of looking at like one to 10 examples, you look at on the order of dozens or even hundreds of examples. This like you manually reviewing it using some kind of eval tool or building your own custom data viewer. And then finally, the most scalable version of this is synthesizing all the common mistakes that your app makes into automated evaluations which you can just scale to n scale it to whatever you want. You could look at hundreds, thousands, tens of thousands of examples and really lock in and improve your system. But this is a feedback loop. You have some way of organizing your context window. You want to generate outputs from the LLM. Then you want to evaluate those responses. And then whenever it makes a mistake, you want to go back and tweak whether it's your system message or just the overall management of the context window. And this is really important because sure you can follow like generic advice for doing good context engineering or prompt engineering, but ultimately every use case is different. So you're going to have to see how specific techniques work for your specific use case. Finally, we have metaprompting, which is refining prompts with an LLM. This can look many different ways, but one is clarifying. So let's say I have a system prompt and I'm using it with GPT5. Basically, what I can do is take my system prompt, pass it to GPT5, and ask it, "Is there anything unclear about these instructions?" This is kind of like a easy way to make your instructions more clear. A slightly better version of this is having it make corrections to the instructions. For example, you can have your system prompt. You can have some input to the system where the model got it wrong. It basically made a mistake. And then you can take all of this information, pass it to GPT5 and say, "Hey, why did you get this wrong? and what can be improved about the system message to avoid this mistake in the future. This is like another way you can improve your prompts. And then again, you just do this iteratively and eventually you get something that's pretty good. I think we did pretty good on time.
